nodes.name: Parse Callback + Authorize
nodes.notes:
3. Understand which candidate the user approved.

Parses the callback_data string 'approve:<job_id>:<index>' into
its three parts. Also re-checks the user allowlist, even if
someone got hold of our button payload, the user id has to
match.

nodes.name: Acknowledge Click
nodes.notes:
4. Tell Telegram 'we got the tap' immediately.

Telegram UI shows a spinner on the tapped button until we
call answerCallbackQuery. So we send that FIRST, before any
slow work.

continueOnFail is on, if the callback id is stale (user
tapped > 30s after cards arrived), Telegram returns 400. We
ignore and keep going, so the reel still renders.

nodes.name: Approved?
nodes.notes:
5. Did the user approve or reject?

Simple if-check on the parsed action. Approve path continues
to the render pipeline. Reject path sends a short 'got it, no
reel' message and stops.

nodes.name: Load Stored Job
nodes.notes:
7. Pick the approved candidate out of the 3.

Parses the job JSON. If job.status == 'cancelled' (because
user ran /cancel), sends a polite refusal DM and stops, no
wasted ElevenLabs or Remotion bill.

Otherwise, pulls out just the approved candidate's scene plan
and passes it forward with chat_id + job_id for later nodes
to reference.

nodes.name: Notify Rejected
nodes.notes:
Cleanup path when the user rejects a card.

Sends a one-line 'rejected, no reel' reply. Not an error ,
rejection is a valid outcome.

nodes.name: Read Job File
nodes.notes:
6. Load the 3 candidates we saved in WF1.

Reads /project/jobs/<jobId>.json from the shared folder and
returns the content as binary data for the next node to parse.

nodes.name: Sub-Workflow Trigger
nodes.notes: Production entry point. WF1 calls this via Execute Workflow when a Telegram Approve/Reject callback arrives. Telegram only allows one webhook per bot, so WF1 owns the webhook and routes callbacks here via Call WF2 (Render).

nodes.name: Normalize Callback Payload
nodes.notes:
2. Pass the callback data through unchanged.

A safety wrapper. If WF1 wraps the callback_query inside another
object, this node unwraps it so downstream nodes always see
the same shape.

nodes.name: Build TTS Text
nodes.notes:
8. Glue the beats into one narration script.

Each beat has its own text. ElevenLabs needs ONE string to
synthesize. This joins them with spaces, ElevenLabs handles
natural pauses on punctuation.
Also attaches the Nikunj voice id so the TTS step knows whose
voice clone to use.

nodes.name: Bridge: Narration
nodes.notes:
9. Turn text into speech + get per-word timings.

HTTP call to the render-bridge on the host at port 5555, which
calls ElevenLabs' /v1/text-to-speech/.../with-timestamps endpoint.
Bridge writes /project/public/narration-<jobId>.mp3 to disk AND
returns every word's start/end time in the audio.
We use the render-bridge for this (not n8n directly) because
we want the audio file on the SAME filesystem where Remotion
will render from.

nodes.name: Bridge: Captions
nodes.notes:
10. Line up words with beats.

Sends the word timings + beat list to the bridge's /captions
endpoint. The bridge walks through the word stream, matching
each beat's first word to figure out exactly when each beat
starts and ends in the audio. Writes
/project/public/captions-<jobId>.json for Remotion to read.

nodes.name: Build Final Content
nodes.notes:
11. Clean up the scene plan and add the B-roll image.

Three things happen here:
  (a) SANITIZE, Claude sometimes writes hex colors where we
      need palette names, or invents SFX names we don't have.
      This node normalizes everything so Remotion's schema
      validation accepts it.
  (b) FETCH B-ROLL, calls the bridge's /broll endpoint to
      download the article's hero image (og:image). On failure
      we skip; the reel still renders without imagery.
  (c) SYNC PILLS, for CommandPills scenes, figures out when
      each pill's text is spoken in the narration so the scene
      can highlight pills one-by-one in sync with the voice.
Also forces CTAOutro style = 'follow', hides the Hindi·AI
watermark by setting channelLabel, and enables the per-letter
caption magnifier.

nodes.name: Bridge: Content
nodes.notes:
12. Save the final content JSON.

Writes /project/content/<jobId>.json to disk. This is what
Remotion reads as its --props argument.

nodes.name: Bridge: Remotion Render
nodes.notes:
13. Actually render the MP4.

HTTP call to the bridge's /render endpoint, which runs
'node scripts/batch-render.mjs content/<jobId>.json' on the
Windows host. Remotion:
  - spins up headless Chrome
  - composites all the React scenes frame-by-frame (30 fps)
  - mixes in the narration + SFX
  - encodes to MP4 via bundled ffmpeg
Takes 45-70 seconds for a 90-second reel. We give the HTTP
call a 10-minute timeout to account for cold starts.

nodes.name: Bridge: Read MP4
nodes.notes:
14. Fetch the finished MP4 as binary.

GETs the bridge's /mp4/<jobId> endpoint, which reads the file
from disk and streams it back. We receive it as a binary blob
ready for Telegram's sendVideo to upload.

nodes.name: Send Video to Telegram
nodes.notes:
15. Deliver the reel to the user's DM.

Telegram sendVideo, uploads the MP4 directly. Also includes
a caption with the title, source, and job id so you know
which request this came from. Telegram caps bot uploads at
50 MB; our reels average 5-10 MB, well under.

nodes.name: Bridge: Check MP4 Cache
nodes.notes:
Cache short-circuit: ask the render-bridge whether this exact
(job_id + candidate_label) already has a rendered MP4 on disk.

If yes, skip every expensive step (ElevenLabs, Remotion, etc.)
and just re-deliver the cached file via Telegram. This is what
prevents double-tap of Approve from burning API quota twice.

nodes.name: Is Cached?
nodes.notes: Idempotency shortcut. TRUE (top) = this candidate was already rendered before -> replay the cached MP4 (~15s). FALSE (bottom) = first approval -> full narration + captions + render path (~90s). Cache key is <jobId>_<candidateLabel>.

nodes.name: Bridge: Read Cached MP4
nodes.notes:
Fetch the already-rendered MP4 from /project/out/<composite_id>.mp4
as a binary blob. Cheap, no compute, just disk read + HTTP stream.

nodes.name: Send Cached Video to Telegram
nodes.notes:
Cache-hit delivery path. Same MP4, different caption so the user
knows this is a replay rather than a fresh render.
